members.name: no_grad
members.signature: no_grad()

methods.name: __enter__
methods.summary: Enter the context.  Returns self so the value can be bound via `with ... as`.

methods.name: __exit__
methods.summary: Exit the context, restoring any state that was modified on entry.

methods.name: __call__
methods.summary: Forward to the underlying callable (see class docstring).

members.summary: Context manager / decorator that disables gradient tracking.
members.extended:
Inside a `no_grad` scope every op skips the graph-building
bookkeeping that autograd normally performs: no autograd node
is registered, no activations are saved, and the produced
tensors carry `requires_grad=False` regardless of their
inputs. The flag is process-global, so this affects all code
running inside the scope.

Use `no_grad` for inference, for validation loops, and for
parameter updates that should not themselves be differentiable
(e.g. EMA accumulation, optimizer steps if performed outside
an Optimizer object). The savings — fewer Python objects
allocated, no saved activations holding memory, no overhead
on each op — are typically large.

The previous gradient mode is restored on exit (RAII), so
`no_grad` may be safely nested inside `enable_grad`
and vice-versa.

parameters.name: None
parameters.description:
`no_grad` takes no arguments; configure with
`set_grad_enabled` if a dynamic flag is needed.

examples:
As a context manager:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import no_grad
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0], requires_grad=True)
>>> with no_grad():
...     y = x * 2
>>> y.requires_grad
False
As a decorator:
>>> @no_grad()
... def eval_step(x):
...     return (x * x).sum()
notes:
Gradient mode controls whether autograd records the chain rule



$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x}
= \sum_i \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i}
  \cdot \frac{\partial y_i}{\partial x}.
$$

With `no_grad` the graph that this sum is computed over is
never built, so calling `backward` afterwards is a
no-op on tensors produced inside the scope.

attributes.name: _prev
attributes.description:
Internal — saved grad-mode flag captured at `__enter__`
time and restored at `__exit__`.

members.name: enable_grad
members.signature: enable_grad()

methods.name: __enter__
methods.summary: Enter the context.  Returns self so the value can be bound via `with ... as`.

methods.name: __exit__
methods.summary: Exit the context, restoring any state that was modified on entry.

methods.name: __call__
methods.summary: Forward to the underlying callable (see class docstring).

members.summary: Context manager / decorator that (re-)enables gradient tracking.
members.extended:
Counterpart of `no_grad`: turns gradient recording back
on inside the wrapped scope, even when an outer scope has
disabled it. Useful when a small differentiable subroutine
runs inside a larger `no_grad` block — for example, an
inner training step embedded in an outer evaluation loop, or
explicit gradient computation for diagnostic plots inside a
decorated inference function.

The previous gradient mode is restored on exit (RAII), so the
surrounding `no_grad` continues to apply once the inner
scope finishes.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import no_grad, enable_grad
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0], requires_grad=True)
>>> with no_grad():
...     with enable_grad():
...         y = (x * x).sum()
>>> y.requires_grad
True

attributes.name: _prev
attributes.description:
Internal — saved grad-mode flag captured at `__enter__`
time and restored at `__exit__`.

members.name: set_grad_enabled
members.signature: set_grad_enabled(flag: bool)
members.summary: Globally set the autograd gradient-tracking flag.
members.extended:
Imperative counterpart of `no_grad` and
`enable_grad`: flips the process-wide flag without a
`with` block. Useful when the desired state is determined
by configuration (e.g. an inference server that disables
grad once at start-up).

parameters.name: flag
parameters.description:
`True` to enable gradient tracking on subsequent ops,
`False` to disable it. The change persists until
another call to `set_grad_enabled` or until a
context-manager-based override.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import set_grad_enabled, is_grad_enabled
>>> set_grad_enabled(False)
>>> is_grad_enabled()
False
>>> set_grad_enabled(True)
notes:
Unlike the context-manager forms, this function does not
stack previous states — repeated calls simply overwrite the
flag. Pair it with `is_grad_enabled` to save and
restore manually if needed.

members.name: is_grad_enabled
members.signature: is_grad_enabled()
members.summary: Return whether autograd gradient tracking is currently enabled.
members.extended:
Reports the current state of the process-wide flag that
`no_grad`, `enable_grad`, and
`set_grad_enabled` mutate. The result reflects whether
subsequent ops will register autograd nodes.

returns.description:
`True` if gradient tracking is enabled (the default
outside any `no_grad` scope); `False` otherwise.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import no_grad, is_grad_enabled
>>> is_grad_enabled()
True
>>> with no_grad():
...     is_grad_enabled()
False
notes:
The flag is the same Boolean queried by the C++ engine at
op construction time — the result is therefore guaranteed
consistent with the actual behaviour of the next op.

members.name: inference_mode
members.signature: inference_mode()
members.summary: Context manager for inference-time autograd suppression.
members.extended:
Like `no_grad` but reserved for fully read-only
inference paths: the user guarantees that no in-place
mutation of autograd-tracked tensors will occur inside the
scope. In the current Lucid implementation
`inference_mode` is an alias for `no_grad`; the API
exists so that future versions can add stricter optimisations
(e.g. skipping version-counter tracking on in-place ops)
without changing user code.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import inference_mode
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0], requires_grad=True)
>>> with inference_mode():
...     y = x * 3
>>> y.requires_grad
False
notes:
Mathematically equivalent to wrapping the inference forward
pass in



$$
y = f(x), \qquad \text{grad mode} = \text{off},
$$

so no part of $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial \theta$
can be reconstructed afterwards.

members.name: backward
members.signature: backward(tensors: Tensor | list[Tensor], grad_tensors: list[Tensor] | None = None, retain_graph: bool = False, create_graph: bool = False, inputs: list[Tensor] | None = None)
members.summary: Compute gradients of `tensors` w.r.t. the leaf variables in their graph.
members.extended:
Top-level entry point that triggers reverse-mode automatic
differentiation across the computation graph rooted at
`tensors`. For every leaf tensor `x` reachable from
`tensors` whose `requires_grad` is `True`, this function
accumulates $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x$ into
`x.grad`, where $\mathcal{L}$ is the (possibly weighted)
sum of the root tensors.

The chain rule is applied edge-by-edge during a topological
walk of the graph in reverse order, so each intermediate
Jacobian-vector product fires exactly once.

parameters.name: tensors
parameters.description:
Root tensors at which the backward pass starts. When more
than one root is supplied each receives its own seed and the
contributions are summed at every shared leaf.

parameters.name: grad_tensors
parameters.description:
Seed cotangent vectors, one per root tensor and matching the
corresponding root's shape. Required when a root is
non-scalar. Defaults to `ones_like(t)` for each root, which
is correct for scalar losses.

parameters.name: retain_graph
parameters.description:
If `True` the intermediate saved tensors are not freed after
the backward pass, so the same graph can be traversed again.
Necessary when calling `backward` multiple times on
overlapping graphs or when `create_graph` is also `True`.

parameters.name: create_graph
parameters.description:
If `True` the operations performed during backward are
themselves recorded in the graph, enabling higher-order
differentiation (e.g. Hessian-vector products, meta-learning).
Implies stronger memory usage. Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description:
Reserved for the future ability to restrict gradient
accumulation to a specified subset of leaves. Currently
unused.

returns.description:
Gradients are accumulated in-place onto each leaf tensor's
`.grad` attribute. Existing `.grad` values are added to,
not overwritten — call `Tensor.zero_grad` (or the
optimizer's `zero_grad`) between successive backward passes
if accumulation is undesired.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import backward
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], requires_grad=True)
>>> y = (x * x).sum()
>>> backward(y)
>>> x.grad
Tensor([2., 4., 6.])
notes:
Reverse-mode AD computes



$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x}
= \sum_{i}
    \left(
        \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial t_i}
    \right)^{\!\top}
    \frac{\partial t_i}{\partial x},
$$

propagating cotangents $\bar t_i = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial t_i$ from the roots through each saved op contract
until every reachable leaf has received its contribution.

Memory/compute trade-off:

* `retain_graph=False` (default) is the cheapest mode — once
  the walk finishes, every saved tensor is freed.
* `retain_graph=True, create_graph=False` keeps activations
  so the same graph can be traversed again.
* `create_graph=True` additionally records the backward ops
  in a new graph, doubling memory in the worst case but
  enabling $\nabla^2 \mathcal{L}$ and beyond.

members.name: grad
members.signature: grad(outputs: Tensor | list[Tensor], inputs: Tensor | list[Tensor], grad_outputs: list[Tensor] | None = None, retain_graph: bool | None = None, create_graph: bool = False, only_inputs: bool = True, allow_unused: bool = False)
members.summary: Compute gradients of outputs w.r.t. inputs, returning them as a tuple.
members.extended:
The "functional" gradient interface — invoke once to get the partial
derivatives back without touching `.grad` on any leaf tensor.
Useful for higher-order differentiation, gradient-based meta-learning,
or any pattern where you want to use the gradients as input to a new
computation rather than to update parameters in-place.

parameters.name: outputs
parameters.description:
Output tensors to differentiate.  Each must have `requires_grad`
set in the graph that produced it.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description:
Input tensors w.r.t. which gradients are requested.  Each must be
a leaf (or non-leaf with `requires_grad=True` if you want grads
flowing into intermediate nodes).

parameters.name: grad_outputs
parameters.description:
Seed gradients $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial \text{outputs}$
for non-scalar outputs.  If omitted, `outputs` is expected to be
scalar and an implicit `ones_like` seed is used.

parameters.name: retain_graph
parameters.description:
Keep the autograd graph alive after this call so additional
backward passes are possible.  Defaults to `create_graph`.

parameters.name: create_graph
parameters.description:
If `True`, build the autograd graph of the gradient itself so
the returned tensors are differentiable — used by
`gradgradcheck` and other higher-order recipes.

parameters.name: only_inputs
parameters.description:
Reserved for reference-framework compatibility; gradients are
always restricted to the requested `inputs`.

parameters.name: allow_unused
parameters.description:
If `True`, return `None` for any `inputs` entry that lies
outside the computation graph of `outputs`.  Otherwise raise.

returns.description:
One gradient per element of `inputs`, in the same order.
Entries are `None` only when `allow_unused=True` and the
input is disconnected from `outputs`.

examples:
Scalar output — no seed needed:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import grad
>>> x = lucid.randn(3, requires_grad=True)
>>> y = (x * x).sum()
>>> (gx,) = grad(y, [x])
>>> gx                         # equals 2 * x
Tensor([...])
Vector output — explicit seed:
>>> z = x * x
>>> seed = lucid.ones_like(z)
>>> (gx,) = grad(z, [x], grad_outputs=[seed])
Higher-order with create_graph=True:
>>> y = (x ** 3).sum()
>>> (g,) = grad(y, [x], create_graph=True)
>>> (gg,) = grad(g.sum(), [x])    # second derivative: 6x
notes:
Mathematically, `grad` computes the vector-Jacobian product



$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{x}}
\left(\sum_k \text{grad\_outputs}_k \cdot \text{outputs}_k\right)
$$

via one reverse-mode pass.  Unlike `Tensor.backward`, it does
NOT accumulate into `.grad` — leaf tensors' existing `.grad`
values are preserved across the call.  For chained gradient
computations (Hessian-vector products, MAML inner loops, etc.) this
is the right primitive.

members.name: Function
members.signature: Function()

methods.name: forward
methods.summary: Compute the forward result of the custom op.
methods.extended:
Subclasses override this static method. Save anything needed
during backward on `ctx` (via `ctx.save_for_backward(...)`
or by setting attributes like `ctx.shape = x.shape`); do not
capture tensors via closure.

parameters.name: ctx
parameters.description: Per-call context; will be passed unchanged to `backward`.

parameters.name: *args
parameters.description:
Positional inputs to the custom op. Non-Tensor positional
arguments are allowed but receive no gradients.

methods.name: backward
methods.summary: Compute gradients of the loss w.r.t. each input of `forward`.
methods.extended:
Subclasses override this static method. The returned tuple must
contain one gradient per **positional argument** of `forward`,
in the same order; non-Tensor arguments receive `None`.

parameters.name: ctx
parameters.description: The same context that was populated during `forward`.

parameters.name: *grad_outputs
parameters.description:
Upstream gradients $\partial L / \partial y_i$, one per
output that `forward` returned.

returns.description:
Downstream gradients matching the positional inputs of
`forward`. Use `None` for inputs that have no gradient.

methods.name: apply
methods.summary: Run the function — autograd-tracked when `forward` requests grad.
methods.extended:
`FunctionMeta` rewrites this on every concrete subclass to dispatch
through `forward`/`backward`; the base implementation here exists
only so `Function.apply` is a real attribute (matters for tooling
and `hasattr` checks).  Calling it on the base class is meaningless
and raises.

members.summary: Base class for custom differentiable operations.
members.extended:
Subclass to define an operation whose forward and backward
passes Lucid's autograd cannot deduce automatically — for
example, an operation that wraps an external library, an op
with a custom backward formula for numerical stability, or a
piecewise-defined function whose gradient differs from naïve
autograd.

Define `forward` and `backward` as
`@staticmethod` on the subclass, then invoke the op via
`apply` (NOT by calling `forward` directly — that
would skip autograd registration).

parameters.name: None
parameters.description:
`Function` itself is never instantiated. Subclasses
are stateless — their behaviour is defined entirely by
the `forward` / `backward` static methods, and ops
are invoked through the `apply` classmethod
rather than `__init__`.

examples:
Custom ReLU with an explicit backward:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import Function
>>> class MyReLU(Function):
...     @staticmethod
...     def forward(ctx, x):
...         ctx.save_for_backward(x)
...         return x.clamp(min=0.0)
...     @staticmethod
...     def backward(ctx, grad_out):
...         (x,) = ctx.saved_tensors
...         return grad_out * (x > 0).float()
>>> y = MyReLU.apply(lucid.tensor([-1.0, 2.0], requires_grad=True))
>>> y.sum().backward()
notes:
The `FunctionCtx` passed to `forward` carries the
bookkeeping autograd needs to call `backward` later — saved
tensors plus per-input non-differentiable flags.

Each `Function` defines a node in the computation graph



$$
\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_n; \theta)
$$

and supplies the chain-rule contribution



$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{x}_i}
= \sum_j \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_j}
  \cdot \frac{\partial y_j}{\partial x_{i}}
$$

for each input $\mathbf{x}_i$. The `backward` method
implements precisely this Jacobian-vector product.

attributes.name: forward
attributes.description:
`forward(ctx, *args, **kwargs) -> Tensor or tuple[Tensor, ...]`.
Computes the primal value; stores anything `backward` will
need on `ctx`.

attributes.name: backward
attributes.description:
`backward(ctx, *grad_outputs) -> tuple of Tensors (or None per
non-tensor input)`. Returns the cotangents matching the
positional inputs of `forward`.

attributes.name: apply
attributes.description:
Bind `forward` to the autograd graph, returning the forward
output and registering `backward` as the gradient closure.

members.name: FunctionCtx
members.signature: FunctionCtx()

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise an empty context with no saved tensors or extras.

methods.name: save_for_backward
methods.summary: Store tensors needed to compute the backward pass.

parameters.name: *tensors
parameters.description:
Activations / inputs the `backward` implementation will
require. They are retrieved later via the `saved_tensors`
property as a tuple in the same order.

notes: Each call replaces any tensors previously saved on this context.

methods.name: saved_tensors
methods.summary: Read back the tensors saved during `forward`.

returns.description:
The tensors stored by `save_for_backward`, wrapped
back into Python `Tensor` instances if the engine stored
raw `TensorImpl` handles.

methods.name: mark_non_differentiable
methods.summary: Declare that the given output tensors carry no gradient.

parameters.name: *tensors
parameters.description:
Outputs of `Function.forward` for which autograd should
not propagate gradients (e.g., integer indices, masks).

members.summary:
Per-call context shared between `Function.forward` and
`Function.backward`.
members.extended:
A fresh `FunctionCtx` is created on every `Function.apply`
invocation. `forward` populates it with anything `backward`
will need: saved tensors (via `save_for_backward`),
non-differentiable output markers (via
`mark_non_differentiable`), and arbitrary user-defined
attributes (cached shapes, axis indices, scalar hyperparameters,
...) set with ordinary attribute assignment. The context is the
only legal channel for passing state from forward to backward —
capturing tensors through Python closures bypasses autograd's
bookkeeping and leaks memory.

parameters.name: None
parameters.description:
`FunctionCtx` is instantiated by `Function.apply`
with no arguments. User code never constructs one
directly; it receives the instance as the first
positional argument of `forward` / `backward`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import Function
>>> class Square(Function):
...     @staticmethod
...     def forward(ctx, x):
...         ctx.save_for_backward(x)
...         ctx.shape = x.shape
...         return x * x
...     @staticmethod
...     def backward(ctx, grad_out):
...         (x,) = ctx.saved_tensors
...         return 2 * x * grad_out
notes:
The context is what ties the forward and backward halves of a
custom node together in the chain rule:



$$
\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{x}; \text{ctx}),
\qquad
\bar{\mathbf{x}} = g(\mathbf{ctx}, \bar{\mathbf{y}}),
$$

where $\bar{\mathbf{y}} = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial \mathbf{y}$ is the upstream gradient and the same
`ctx` object is passed to both halves so $g$ can read
back whatever $f$ saved.

attributes.name: needs_input_grad
attributes.description:
One flag per positional `Tensor` input to `forward`,
indicating whether autograd would propagate a gradient to
that input. Use this to skip unneeded branches in
`backward`.

attributes.name: saved_tensors
attributes.description:
Read-only view of the tensors stored via
`save_for_backward`, in registration order.

members.name: gradcheck
members.signature: gradcheck(func: Callable[..., Tensor | tuple[Tensor, ...]], inputs: Sequence[Tensor], eps: float = 1e-06, atol: float = 1e-05, rtol: float = 0.001, raise_exception: bool = True)
members.summary: Compare analytical gradients from backward() against finite-difference Jacobians.
members.extended: Implemented entirely with the Lucid C++ engine — no numpy.

parameters.name: func
parameters.description: Scalar-valued function mapping Tensor inputs to a single-element Tensor.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description: Inputs to func that require grad (must be float tensors).

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Finite-difference step size (default 1e-6).

parameters.name: atol
parameters.description: Tolerances for the gradient comparison.

parameters.name: rtol
parameters.description: Tolerances for the gradient comparison.

parameters.name: raise_exception
parameters.description: Raise AssertionError on mismatch (default True), else return False.

returns.annotation: bool  True if all gradients agree within tolerance.

members.name: gradgradcheck
members.signature: gradgradcheck(func: Callable[..., Tensor | tuple[Tensor, ...]], inputs: Sequence[Tensor], grad_outputs: Sequence[Tensor] | None = None, eps: float = 1e-06, atol: float = 1e-05, rtol: float = 0.001, raise_exception: bool = True)
members.summary: Verify second-order gradients via finite differences.
members.extended:
Most bugs in custom `lucid.autograd.Function.backward`
implementations show up at the *second*-derivative level —
the first-order gradient is consistent but the gradient of
the gradient is not. `gradgradcheck` constructs such a
test by wrapping `func` in a scalar-valued helper

$$
\tilde f(x) = \sum_i (\nabla f(x))_i,
$$

differentiates it analytically with
`create_graph=True`, and then runs `gradcheck` on
$\tilde f$ so its gradient is compared against the
central finite-difference estimate

$$
\frac{\tilde f(x + \varepsilon e_k)
      - \tilde f(x - \varepsilon e_k)}{2 \varepsilon}
\approx \frac{\partial^2 f(x)}{\partial x_k^2}.
$$

Disagreement signals a bug in the analytic backward
formula that ordinary `gradcheck` would miss.

parameters.name: func
parameters.description:
Function mapping `Tensor` inputs to a `Tensor` (or
tuple of `Tensor`). Must be twice differentiable.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description:
Input tensors at which to verify the gradient. Floating
dtype required.

parameters.name: grad_outputs
parameters.description:
Reserved for custom upstream gradients in the inner
backward pass. Currently ignored — `ones_like`
upstream gradients are always used.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description:
Finite-difference step size used by the underlying
`gradcheck`. Defaults to `1e-6`.

parameters.name: atol
parameters.description:
Absolute tolerance for the comparison. Defaults to
`1e-5`.

parameters.name: rtol
parameters.description:
Relative tolerance for the comparison. Defaults to
`1e-3`.

parameters.name: raise_exception
parameters.description:
If `True` (default) raise `AssertionError` on
mismatch; if `False` return `False` silently.

returns.description:
`True` iff all second-order gradients agree with the
finite-difference reference within the supplied
tolerances.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import gradgradcheck
>>> x = lucid.randn(3, requires_grad=True, dtype=lucid.float64)
>>> def f(x):
...     return (x ** 3).sum()
>>> gradgradcheck(f, [x])
True
notes:
The bound on the truncation error of central differences is



$$
\left|
    \frac{\tilde f(x + \varepsilon) - \tilde f(x - \varepsilon)}
         {2 \varepsilon}
    - \tilde f'(x)
\right|
= O(\varepsilon^2),
$$

so tightening `eps` improves accuracy until round-off
error dominates.

members.name: detect_anomaly
members.signature: detect_anomaly(check_nan: bool = True)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the instance.  See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: __enter__
methods.summary: Enter the context.  Returns self so the value can be bound via `with ... as`.

methods.name: __exit__
methods.summary: Exit the context, restoring any state that was modified on entry.

methods.name: __call__
methods.summary: Support use as a decorator.

members.summary: Context manager / decorator that enables autograd anomaly detection.
members.extended:
Reverse-mode automatic differentiation propagates a chain of partial
derivatives backwards through the computation graph; a single
`NaN` or `Inf` appearing anywhere in that chain silently
contaminates every upstream gradient and is one of the hardest
classes of training bugs to localise. `detect_anomaly` opts the
current scope into stricter checking: after each call to
`backward` the resulting gradients are scanned for
non-finite values, and an exception is raised at the offending
boundary so the failure surfaces at the source rather than at the
optimiser step.

Enabling anomaly detection adds overhead — every backward pass
performs an additional reduction over the gradient tensors — so it
is intended for debugging sessions, not steady-state training.

parameters.name: check_nan
parameters.description:
If `True` (default) every backward pass is checked for
`NaN` / `Inf` gradients and a `RuntimeError` is raised on
the first violation. Setting it to `False` enters the context
but performs no checking — useful for nested scopes where an
outer block already enabled the flag.

examples:
Use as a context manager during loss computation:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import detect_anomaly
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], requires_grad=True)
>>> with detect_anomaly():
...     y = (x * x).sum()
...     y.backward()
Use as a decorator on a training step:
>>> @detect_anomaly()
... def train_step(x, target):
...     loss = ((x - target) ** 2).sum()
...     loss.backward()
...     return loss
notes:
Reverse-mode AD computes



$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x}
= \sum_{p \in \text{paths}(x \to \mathcal{L})}
    \prod_{(u, v) \in p} \frac{\partial v}{\partial u}.
$$

A single non-finite Jacobian entry along any path corrupts the
sum, so the check is performed on the final accumulated gradient
rather than on individual op outputs.

The context manager restores the previous anomaly flag on exit,
so nested `with` blocks behave correctly.

attributes.name: check_nan
attributes.description: The mode argument as passed to `__init__`.

members.name: is_anomaly_enabled
members.signature: is_anomaly_enabled()
members.summary: Return whether autograd anomaly detection is currently enabled.
members.extended:
Query accessor for the global flag that `detect_anomaly`
and `set_detect_anomaly` mutate. Useful when conditionally
wrapping additional debug logic, or when asserting state inside
test suites.

returns.description:
`True` if anomaly detection is currently active; `False`
otherwise.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import detect_anomaly, is_anomaly_enabled
>>> is_anomaly_enabled()
False
>>> with detect_anomaly():
...     is_anomaly_enabled()
True
>>> is_anomaly_enabled()
False
notes:
The flag is process-global. In multi-threaded code (Lucid does
not currently run multiple Python threads through the autograd
engine) the value would be shared across threads — treat it as
a debug switch rather than per-call state.

members.name: set_detect_anomaly
members.signature: set_detect_anomaly(mode: bool, check_nan: bool = True)
members.summary: Programmatic global toggle for autograd anomaly detection.
members.extended:
Equivalent to entering `detect_anomaly` once and never
exiting — flips the process-wide flag that `backward`
consults at the end of each pass. Prefer the `with`-block form
when the scope is bounded; use this free function when the flag
is controlled by a CLI argument or an environment variable read
once at start-up.

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description:
`True` enables `NaN` / `Inf` checking on every backward;
`False` disables it.

parameters.name: check_nan
parameters.description:
Forwarded to the underlying flag. Currently the only supported
check is for non-finite values; the parameter exists for API
symmetry and forward compatibility.

returns.description:
The function mutates a module-level flag; it has no return
value.

examples:
Enable checks at start-up:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import set_detect_anomaly, is_anomaly_enabled
>>> set_detect_anomaly(True)
>>> is_anomaly_enabled()
True
>>> set_detect_anomaly(False)
notes:
The effective flag is $\text{mode} \land \text{check\_nan}$:
setting either to `False` disables checking. There is no stack of
saved states — once changed, the flag stays until the next call.

members.name: jacobian
members.signature: jacobian(func: Callable[..., Tensor], inputs: Tensor | tuple[Tensor, ...], create_graph: bool = False, strict: bool = False, vectorize: bool = False)
members.summary: Compute the Jacobian matrix of `func` with respect to each input.
members.extended:
The Jacobian of a vector-valued function
$f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$ is

$$
J_{ij} = \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_j}, \qquad
J \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}.
$$

Lucid evaluates it row-by-row by repeated reverse-mode
backward passes — one per output element — seeding each pass
with a one-hot cotangent so the resulting input gradient is
exactly the corresponding Jacobian row. The cost therefore
scales with the output dimension $m$; prefer `vjp`
when only $v^\top J$ is needed and `jvp` when only
$J v$ is needed.

parameters.name: func
parameters.description:
A function mapping `Tensor` inputs to a `Tensor` (or
tuple of `Tensor`). Must be differentiable w.r.t. each
positional input.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description:
Input tensor(s) at which the Jacobian is evaluated. They
are silently promoted to `requires_grad=True` if needed.

parameters.name: create_graph
parameters.description:
If `True` the Jacobian itself is differentiable, enabling
higher-order derivatives (e.g. building `hessian` on
top). Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: strict
parameters.description:
Reserved for stricter shape/dtype validation. Currently
unused.

parameters.name: vectorize
parameters.description:
Reserved for a future vmap-based implementation. Currently
unused.

returns.description:
For a single input `x` the returned tensor has shape
`(prod(out_shape), prod(x.shape))`. For multiple inputs
a tuple is returned, one Jacobian block per input.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import jacobian
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
>>> def f(x):
...     return x * x
>>> J = jacobian(f, x)
>>> J.shape
(3, 3)
notes:
Reverse-mode differentiation makes the cost per row
$O(\text{cost}(f))$; the full Jacobian therefore costs
$O(m \cdot \text{cost}(f))$. For square or wide
Jacobians ($m \ge n$) forward-mode would be cheaper —
Lucid does not yet ship a forward-mode implementation, so
this routine is preferred for tall Jacobians
($m \ll n$).

members.name: hessian
members.signature: hessian(func: Callable[..., Tensor], inputs: Tensor | tuple[Tensor, ...], create_graph: bool = False, strict: bool = False, vectorize: bool = False)
members.summary: Compute the Hessian matrix of a scalar-valued `func`.
members.extended:
The Hessian of a scalar function
$f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is

$$
H_{ij} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \, \partial x_j},
\qquad H \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}.
$$

Implemented as `jacobian` of the gradient of `func` —
a forward pass produces the loss, a first backward (with
`create_graph=True`) builds the gradient graph, and a second
backward along each gradient coordinate yields the rows of
$H$. Cost is therefore $O(n \cdot \text{cost}(\nabla f))$.

parameters.name: func
parameters.description: Scalar-valued function of one or more `Tensor` inputs.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description:
Inputs at which $H$ is evaluated. They are silently
promoted to `requires_grad=True` if necessary.

parameters.name: create_graph
parameters.description:
If `True` the Hessian itself remains differentiable
(third-order derivatives). Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: strict
parameters.description: Reserved for stricter validation. Currently unused.

parameters.name: vectorize
parameters.description:
Reserved for a future vmap-based implementation. Currently
unused.

returns.annotation: Tensor or tuple of tuple of Tensor
returns.description:
For a single input the returned tensor has shape
`(numel(x), numel(x))`. For multiple inputs a nested
tuple of cross-Hessian blocks is returned, with
`H[i][j]` containing $\partial^2 f / (\partial x_i \, \partial x_j)$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import hessian
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0])
>>> def f(x):
...     return (x * x).sum()
>>> H = hessian(f, x)
>>> H.shape
(2, 2)
notes:
Symmetry $H_{ij} = H_{ji}$ holds in exact arithmetic
when $f$ is $C^2$. In floating-point the result is
only approximately symmetric; symmetrize as
$\tfrac{1}{2}(H + H^\top)$ if a strictly symmetric
matrix is required.

members.name: vjp
members.signature: vjp(func: Callable[..., Tensor], inputs: Tensor | tuple[Tensor, ...], v: Tensor | tuple[Tensor, ...], create_graph: bool = False, strict: bool = False)
members.summary: Vector-Jacobian product $v^\top J$ (reverse-mode AD).
members.extended:
Given $f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$ with Jacobian
$J \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ and a cotangent vector
$v \in \mathbb{R}^m$, returns

$$
v^\top J \in \mathbb{R}^{n}
$$

along with the primal output $y = f(x)$. This is the
operation that backpropagation performs on every node: when
a scalar loss $\mathcal{L}(y)$ is being differentiated
against an intermediate $y$, the upstream cotangent is
$v = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial y$ and the result
is $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x$.

Computing a full VJP costs the same as one backward pass —
much cheaper than materialising $J$ when only the
product is needed.

parameters.name: func
parameters.description:
Function mapping `Tensor` inputs to a `Tensor` (or
tuple thereof).

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description:
Primal point $x$ at which $J$ is evaluated.
Silently promoted to `requires_grad=True` if needed.

parameters.name: v
parameters.description:
Cotangent vector(s) matching the output shape(s) of
`func`. Scalar-valued `v` is broadcast for scalar
outputs.

parameters.name: create_graph
parameters.description:
If `True` the returned VJP is itself differentiable,
enabling double-backward. Defaults to `False`.

parameters.name: strict
parameters.description: Reserved for stricter validation. Currently unused.

returns.annotation: tuple of (Tensor, tuple of (Tensor or None))
returns.description:
`(output, vjp_grads)` where `output = func(*inputs)`
and `vjp_grads[i]` is $v^\top J$ projected onto
input `i` (or `None` if that input has no gradient
path).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import vjp
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
>>> v = lucid.tensor([1.0, 1.0, 1.0])
>>> def f(x):
...     return x * x
>>> y, (grad_x,) = vjp(f, x, v)
notes:
The dual to `vjp` is `jvp`, which computes
$J v$ via forward-mode (or finite differences in
Lucid's current implementation).

members.name: jvp
members.signature: jvp(func: Callable[..., Tensor], inputs: Tensor | tuple[Tensor, ...], v: Tensor | tuple[Tensor, ...], create_graph: bool = False, strict: bool = False)
members.summary: Jacobian-vector product $J v$ (forward-mode directional derivative).
members.extended:
Given $f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$ with Jacobian
$J \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ and a tangent vector
$v \in \mathbb{R}^n$, returns

$$
J v = \left.\frac{d}{dt} f(x + t v)\right|_{t=0}
    \in \mathbb{R}^{m}
$$

along with the primal output $y = f(x)$. JVPs are the
natural primitive of forward-mode AD and are useful for
propagating tangent information (sensitivities) through a
network in a single forward sweep, for computing directional
derivatives, and as a building block for second-order methods.

Lucid currently realises the JVP via a symmetric central
finite difference

$$
J v \approx \frac{f(x + \varepsilon v) - f(x - \varepsilon v)}
                 {2 \varepsilon},
$$

with $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$. This avoids the need for a
true forward-mode implementation while still being accurate
enough for testing and most applications.

parameters.name: func
parameters.description:
Function mapping `Tensor` inputs to a `Tensor` (or
tuple thereof).

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description: Primal point $x$.

parameters.name: v
parameters.description: Tangent vector(s) matching the input shape(s).

parameters.name: create_graph
parameters.description:
Reserved for the future native forward-mode implementation.
Currently unused.

parameters.name: strict
parameters.description: Reserved for stricter validation. Currently unused.

returns.annotation: tuple of (Tensor or tuple of Tensor, Tensor or tuple of Tensor)
returns.description:
`(primals_out, tangents_out)` where
`primals_out = func(*inputs)` and `tangents_out` has the
same shape as `primals_out` and holds $J v$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.autograd import jvp
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
>>> v = lucid.tensor([1.0, 0.0, 0.0])
>>> def f(x):
...     return x * x
>>> y, tangent = jvp(f, x, v)
notes:
The complementary operation is `vjp`, which computes
$v^\top J$ cheaply via reverse-mode. Use `jvp`
when the input dimension is small relative to the output
dimension; otherwise reverse-mode is more efficient.

members.name: checkpoint
members.signature: checkpoint(function: Callable[..., Tensor], args: Tensor = (), preserve_rng_state: bool = True, use_reentrant: bool = True, kwargs: object = {})
members.summary: Run *function* under gradient checkpointing.
members.extended:
Executes `function(*args, **kwargs)` during the forward pass
**without** tracking intermediate activations (`no_grad` context).
During the backward pass the function is re-executed under
`enable_grad` to reconstruct the local autograd graph, and the
gradients are computed through that graph.

parameters.name: function
parameters.description:
The differentiable segment to checkpoint.  Must accept tensors as
positional arguments and return a single `lucid.Tensor`.

parameters.name: *args
parameters.description: Positional tensor inputs to *function*.

parameters.name: preserve_rng_state
parameters.description:
Accepted for API compatibility.  RNG state restoration is not yet
implemented — set to `False` when *function* contains stochastic
layers.

parameters.name: use_reentrant
parameters.description:
Accepted for API compatibility.  Only the reentrant (default)
implementation is provided.

parameters.name: **kwargs
parameters.description:
Extra keyword arguments forwarded to *function* on both the
forward and recomputation passes.

examples:
>>> def block(x):
...     return lucid.nn.functional.relu(x @ W + b)
>>> y = lucid.autograd.checkpoint(block, x)
>>> y.sum().backward()

members.name: RemovableHandle
members.signature: RemovableHandle(hooks_list: list[Callable[..., object]], hook: Callable[..., object])

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the instance.  See the class docstring for parameter semantics.

methods.name: remove
methods.summary: Remove the hook from the tensor.

methods.name: __enter__
methods.summary: Enter the context.  Returns self so the value can be bound via `with ... as`.

methods.name: __exit__
methods.summary: Exit the context, restoring any state that was modified on entry.

members.summary: Handle returned by `lucid.Tensor.register_hook`.
members.extended:
A `RemovableHandle` keeps a reference to the list of hooks
attached to a tensor's gradient and to the specific callable
that was just registered, providing two ways to take the hook
back off:

* Imperative — call `remove` whenever the hook is no
  longer needed.
* RAII — bind the handle in a `with` block; the hook is
  automatically removed when control leaves the block.

This class is *distinct* from
`lucid.nn.hooks.RemovableHandle`: that one manages
forward/backward hooks on `lucid.nn.Module`, whereas
this one operates on the per-tensor hooks dispatched from
`lucid.Tensor.backward` after the gradient has been
accumulated.

parameters.name: hooks_list
parameters.description:
The underlying registry list owned by the tensor; on
`remove` the hook is dropped from this list.

parameters.name: hook
parameters.description:
The exact callable to remove. Identity (`is`) is used
for matching.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0], requires_grad=True)
>>> handle = x.register_hook(lambda g: print('grad =', g))
>>> (x * x).sum().backward()
>>> handle.remove()
As a context manager:
>>> with x.register_hook(lambda g: g * 2) as h:
...     (x * x).sum().backward()
notes:
The post-backward hook contract is



$$
\bar x \leftarrow h(\bar x),
$$

where $\bar x = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x$ is
the accumulated gradient and $h$ is the user-supplied
hook. A hook returning `None` leaves $\bar x$
unchanged; returning a tensor replaces it.

`remove` is idempotent — calling it twice is harmless.
